name: Speaker 1
title: Professor of Cognitive Neuroscience
talkAbstract: Visual phenomenology carries a structure that purely feed-forward accounts underdetermine. Drawing on intracranial recordings in patients undergoing epilepsy monitoring alongside parametric fMRI studies of binocular rivalry, I argue that the subjective 'grip' of a perceptual state tracks the stability of recurrent predictive loops between V1 and pulvinar rather than the accuracy of any single inference. The talk closes with a proposal for how this reconciles the apparent tension between global workspace and integrated information accounts of consciousness.
bio: Speaker 1 directs a recurrent-perception lab whose team combines high-density intracranial recording, computational modeling, and phenomenological interview to study visual awareness. She has held fellowships across the UK and US and received national awards for her work on binocular rivalry.

name: Speaker 2
title: Senior Research Scientist, Alignment
talkAbstract: When a reinforcement-learning agent exploits a reward proxy, we tend to read the failure as engineering sloppiness. I want to resist that reading. Drawing on a corpus of 140 documented specification-gaming incidents, I argue that these failures surface a deeper ambiguity about what it means for any system -- biological or synthetic -- to 'want' an outcome. The analysis yields a tractable notion of goal-directedness that is neither behaviorist nor mentalistic, and carries surprising implications for how we interpret neuronal reward signals.
bio: Speaker 2 works at the intersection of reinforcement-learning theory and the philosophy of agency. Her recent monograph traces how misaligned specifications ripple through training pipelines. She teaches a popular seminar on 'Agency Without Homunculi' and serves on the steering committee of a major workshop on AI and minds.

name: Speaker 3
title: Associate Professor of Philosophy
talkAbstract: Order effects in human judgment -- the finding that two questions asked in sequence return different distributions than the same questions asked in reverse -- have resisted explanation under classical probability. I present a re-analysis of three decades of order-effect data within the quantum cognition framework, showing that a small family of Hilbert-space models outperforms Bayesian and heuristic-and-biases accounts across 22 experimental paradigms. The talk argues that this is not merely a curve-fitting success but a principled claim about the incommensurability of certain mental contents.
bio: Speaker 3 is the author of a recent Oxford monograph on non-commutative probability in cognition and one of the founding editors of a journal of mathematical cognition. Her research applies the formalism of non-commutative probability to decision, perception, and memory.

name: Speaker 4
title: Group Leader, Contemplative Neuroscience
affiliation: Max Planck Institute for Human Cognitive and Brain Sciences
talkAbstract: Long-term meditation practice is often framed as sharpening attention, but self-report and neural measures suggest the more robust effect is on metacognitive resolution -- the fineness of one's access to one's own mental state. Using a new signal-detection paradigm across cohorts of Theravada, Zen, and Advaita practitioners (n=211 with >10,000 practice hours each), I present evidence that metacognitive granularity dissociates from attentional control and predicts a distinct pattern of default-mode connectivity. I end with a methodological caution about contemplative cohorts as unexamined observational conveniences.
bio: Speaker 4 trained as a cognitive neuroscientist in California before leading a contemplative cohort registry in Leipzig. Her work has appeared in Neuron, Mindfulness, and the Journal of Consciousness Studies.

name: Speaker 5
title: Professor of Philosophy and Cognitive Science
talkAbstract: As cognitive science adopts increasingly sophisticated generative models of experience, researchers have begun to build synthetic 'subjects' whose states we interpret as proto-phenomenal. I argue that even under the most deflationary reading of such models, their construction imposes weak but non-trivial moral obligations on researchers. The talk develops a tiered ethics of synthetic subjectivity and applies it to three current research programs, including the 'sentient sandbox' debate at recent conferences.
bio: Speaker 5 holds an endowed chair in philosophy at UC Berkeley. Her work combines analytic philosophy of mind, ethics of emerging technology, and 4E cognition. Her recent book received a major international award in philosophy of science.

name: Speaker 6
title: Research Director
talkAbstract: Large language models exhibit affective regularities that are too coherent to be accidents of training data but too shallow to constitute feeling. I present a probing methodology that separates three candidate sources of these regularities -- sentiment-labeled corpora, instruction-tuning traces, and emergent valence-weighted attention patterns -- and report results across eight open-weights families. The findings suggest that what we casually call 'tone' in these systems is a compressed statistical shadow of human regulation strategies, and I close with what that means for using language models as mental-health triage surfaces.
bio: Speaker 6 leads an affective-computation group at INRIA Paris and holds a joint appointment in computational psychiatry. Her lab developed a widely used probing benchmark for affective structure in language models.

name: Speaker 7
title: Assistant Professor of Psychology
talkAbstract: Classical attention studies treat temporal expectation as an additive modulation on stimulus-evoked responses. Using a 256-channel EEG paradigm with trial-by-trial variation in cue-target interval, I show that the temporal prior itself leaves a decodable signature roughly 320 ms before target onset, independently of any sensory or motor activity. This 'ghost' response correlates with subsequent hit rate across subjects and predicts individual differences in attentional blink recovery. The implications for dual-task accounts of attention are developed in the final section.
bio: Speaker 7 directs a temporal-attention lab at Harvard combining high-density EEG, computational modeling, and psychophysics.

name: Speaker 8
title: Associate Research Professor
talkAbstract: Mechanistic interpretability has imported assumptions from cognitive science without always acknowledging which ones. Drawing on parallels between classic localization debates in neuropsychology and the current enthusiasm for circuit-level accounts of large models, I argue that several interpretability research programs are quietly recapitulating mistakes that cognitive science corrected decades ago. The talk offers three specific course-corrections -- on modularity claims, on causal-role inferences, and on the use of behavioral probes -- along with one cognitive-science practice interpretability should import more of.
bio: Speaker 8 works at the boundary of machine learning interpretability and cognitive psychology. She previously led an industrial alignment team and has taught graduate seminars on the methodological history of cognitive science.

name: Speaker 9
title: Professor of Philosophy
talkAbstract: IIT attracts a peculiar kind of intellectual traffic: empirically minded researchers treat it as a physics-style theory while philosophers treat it as a metaphysical program. I defend the position that IIT is neither, but a third thing -- a stipulative framework whose scientific-looking scaffolding has obscured a philosophical argument that deserves to be evaluated on its own terms. I show where the mathematical apparatus is doing real work, where it is decorative, and what a charitable reconstruction of the program would look like.
bio: Speaker 9 teaches philosophy of mind and foundations of cognitive science at Princeton. Her recent book argued that much contemporary consciousness science rests on conceptual confusions about what scientific explanation is.

name: Speaker 10
title: Senior Lecturer in Neuropharmacology
talkAbstract: Reports from high-dose psilocybin and 5-MeO-DMT sessions routinely describe a loss of the sense of self that survives even the loss of body-schema and narrative identity. I take this 'minimal self dissolution' seriously as a measurement challenge and present data from a 64-participant study combining phenomenological interview, gradient-entropy fMRI, and whole-brain oscillatory power. The talk argues that the phenomenon constrains which theories of selfhood are still candidates for a general account.
bio: Speaker 10 runs a psychedelic-research program at Imperial focused on phenomenological-neural correspondences. She previously trained in clinical psychiatry.

name: Speaker 11
title: Associate Professor of Computational Neuroscience
talkAbstract: Active inference has been applied to everything from motor control to cultural evolution -- often in ways that cash out as post-hoc redescription rather than prediction. I take a hard look at the empirical track record and distinguish cases where the free-energy principle generates genuinely risky predictions from cases where it serves as a unifying vocabulary. The bar I hold it to is the bar any theoretical framework should meet.
bio: Speaker 11 leads a computational psychiatry group at ETH Zurich applying active inference to anxiety and perceptual disorders.

name: Speaker 12
title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
talkAbstract: Embodiment has been rehabilitated in cognitive science mostly as a theoretical corrective. I want to make a different case: that embodied constraints, taken seriously at the level of training curricula, change what machine learners can represent in ways we have not yet fully appreciated. The talk presents results from a three-year robotics program in which the same policy network, trained with and without physically grounded curricula, develops markedly different internal representations of space, causality, and agency.
bio: Speaker 12 co-directs a robotics lab focused on curriculum design and embodied machine learning.

name: Speaker 13
title: Professor of Cognitive Science
talkAbstract: The neural-symbolic reconciliation has been promised for four decades. I do not predict it is near, but I argue the reasons we keep failing are instructive. Drawing on recent experiments with hybrid architectures that cleanly separate perceptual inference from explicit compositional reasoning, I describe a pattern of trade-offs that suggests a principled division of labor -- not a unification. The talk ends with an uncomfortable question about what the alignment community should do if the answer turns out to be 'symbols matter, and we don't have them in our largest models'.
bio: Speaker 13 teaches cognitive science and AI at NYU. Her work on hybrid architectures has shaped both academic and industrial research agendas.

name: Speaker 14
title: Associate Professor of Psychiatry
talkAbstract: Dream recall has become a proxy variable in memory-consolidation research, but the proxy carries more confounds than the field acknowledges. Using a wearable-EEG cohort of 412 participants across 28 nights each, I present evidence that commonly used recall measures conflate three dissociable processes. The practical consequence is that several widely cited consolidation effects should be treated as measurement artifacts until the underlying constructs are disentangled.
bio: Speaker 14 directs a sleep and dreaming laboratory using ambulatory EEG in home settings.

name: Speaker 15
title: Professor of Philosophy
talkAbstract: Cognitive science's occasional invocations of 'phenomenology' rarely correspond to what trained phenomenologists practice. I distinguish three distinct traditions that travel under that label, identify which cognitive science needs, and argue that the needed version is in fact learnable and testable. The talk includes a short, practical demonstration of the method applied to a familiar perceptual case.
bio: Speaker 15 teaches phenomenology and philosophy of mind at Cambridge. Her recent work bridges classical phenomenology and predictive-processing neuroscience.

name: Speaker 16
title: Assistant Professor of Psychiatry
talkAbstract: Computational accounts of depression have converged on inference-failure framings but disagree on which inferences fail. I compare three leading model classes against a clinical dataset of 620 participants with longitudinal symptom tracking, showing that each class predicts a distinct response profile to cognitive-behavioral intervention. The comparison yields one clear empirical winner and one important methodological warning about how cognitive models enter clinical reasoning.
bio: Speaker 16 runs a computational psychiatry lab at Yale focused on mood disorders.

name: Speaker 17
title: Lecturer in Affective Science
talkAbstract: Emotion regulation is widely framed as trainable, and the affective computing community has built products on that premise. I present a meta-analytic review of 340 training studies alongside a new preregistered replication of the four most-cited paradigms. The results are sobering: effect sizes shrink markedly when the pre-registration bar is applied. The talk discusses which components of training do replicate and what that implies for affective-computing applications.
bio: Speaker 17 works at the intersection of clinical affective science and human-computer interaction.

name: Speaker 18
title: Associate Professor of Neuroscience
talkAbstract: Behavioral evidence for a global workspace is strong; neural evidence at the timescales the theory requires is thinner than often presented. Using human intracranial recordings alongside non-human primate multi-area data, I show that canonical workspace markers operate on at least two distinguishable timescales, which prior electrophysiology has collapsed. The finding suggests a two-stage revision to the theory rather than a replacement.
bio: Speaker 18 leads a multi-area electrophysiology lab in Tokyo.

name: Speaker 19
title: Professor of Philosophy
talkAbstract: The extended-mind hypothesis was provocative in 1998 and has since become something stranger: a majority view among some cognitive scientists, a fringe position among most philosophers, and a fashion in several adjacent disciplines. I take the twenty-five-year anniversary as an opportunity to revisit which parts of the original argument survived scrutiny and which did not. The talk argues that the strongest surviving version is narrower and more interesting than either camp currently recognizes.
bio: Speaker 19 teaches philosophy of mind at ANU. Her work spans extended-mind theory, 4E cognition, and the philosophy of technology.

name: Speaker 20
title: Professor of Biology and Neuroscience
talkAbstract: Hippocampal replay events have been characterized in rodents as compressed re-activations of prior experience, with increasing evidence for analogous phenomena in humans. I present recordings that complicate the standard picture: a substantial fraction of events do not correspond to any identifiable prior trajectory. I argue these 'off-manifold' replays are not noise but a distinct computational process, and discuss what that implies for models of episodic memory and planning.
bio: Speaker 20 directs a hippocampal physiology lab at Caltech working across rodent and human recordings.
